# Verify the --dry-run, --quiet, and --aggressive flags on `sesam clean`.
#
# `leak-src.txt` is force-added to git (sesam's .gitignore would otherwise
# block it) so the test can re-create the leaked plaintext between scenarios.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

exec git add -f leak-src.txt
exec git commit -m 'add leak source'

# --- scenario 1: --dry-run prints but does not delete ---
exec cp leak-src.txt leaked.txt
exec sesam clean --dry-run
stdout 'leaked\.txt'
exists leaked.txt

# --- scenario 2: --quiet stays silent and deletes ---
exec sesam clean --quiet
! stdout '.+'
! exists leaked.txt

# --- scenario 3: without --aggressive, files inside .sesam/ survive ---
mkdir -p .sesam/tmp/scratch
exec cp leak-src.txt .sesam/tmp/scratch/spill
exec cp leak-src.txt leaked.txt
exec sesam clean
! exists leaked.txt
exists .sesam/tmp/scratch/spill

# --- scenario 4: --aggressive also wipes untracked files inside .sesam/ ---
exec cp leak-src.txt leaked.txt
exec sesam clean --aggressive
! exists leaked.txt
! exists .sesam/tmp/scratch/spill

-- leak-src.txt --
leaked plaintext
